FTSE 100 Rallies; UK Inflation Steady Before Iran War

Adnoc CEO tells US vice president that free passage through strait of Hormuz only solution to stabilizing markets
FTSE 100 climbs on Middle East peace deal hopes
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Stocks rallied on Wednesday, and oil prices cooled, on hopes for an end to the conflict in the Middle East despite Iran reportedly rejecting a peace plan proposed by the US.

"Investors are pinning their hopes on a 15-point ceasefire deal unveiled by the US despite the proposals apparently being rebuffed by Iran," said AJ Bell Head of Markets Dan Coatsworth.

The FTSE 100 index closed up 141.68 points, 1.4%, at 10,106.84.

The FTSE 250 ended up 339.68 points, 1.6%, at 21,475.45, while the AIM All-Share leapt 15.15 points, 2.1%, to 729.24.

The Cboe UK 100 was up 1.3% at 1,004.34, the Cboe UK 250 rose 1.1% to 18,609.96, and the Cboe UK Small Companies Index ended 0.8% higher at 16,929.63.

In European equities on Wednesday, the CAC 40 in Paris closed up 1.1%, while the DAX 40 in Frankfurt ended 1.4% higher.

"European markets are following the wider global theme of optimism as rumours swirl around the apparent negotiations that could lead us out of the conflict in Iran," said Joshua Mahony, chief market analyst at Scope Markets.

"Dip buyers have started to grab what they can, while oil prices shift lower as the perceived length of time associated with the disruption shortens."

Stocks in New York were higher. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was up 0.6%, as was the S&P 500 index, while the Nasdaq Composite firmed 1.0%.

Oil prices eased as US President Donald Trump voiced optimism about ending the Middle East war and said officials were "in negotiations right now".

But Iranian state television, citing an unidentified senior official, said that Iran had rejected a peace plan proposed by the US.

"Iran has responded negatively to an American proposal aimed at ending the ongoing imposed war," the official said, according to the English-language broadcaster Press TV.

"The end of the war will occur when Iran decides it should end, not when Trump envisions its conclusion."

There has been no official Iranian statement about the reported peace plan. Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, who led pre-war talks with the US, has yet to comment.

But Iranian media such as the Mehr and Tasnim agencies have picked up the Press TV report.

Earlier, two senior Pakistani officials said that – in an effort to mediate – Islamabad had conveyed to Iran a 15-point plan containing US proposals to end the nearly month-long war.

But a spokesperson for Iran's military said the US was "negotiating with itself", adding: "Someone like us will never come to terms with someone like you."

On Tuesday, US President Donald Trump insisted Iran wants a deal "so badly", saying Tehran had given the US a "very big present worth a tremendous amount of money".

Brent oil was quoted at USD100.91 a barrel at the time of the London equities close on Wednesday, down from USD103.95 late Tuesday.

Morgan Stanley upgraded the oil sector to 'attractive' from 'in line', and raised BP to 'overweight' from 'equal weight', although it downgraded Shell to 'equal weight' from 'overweight.

BP rose 1.3% in London, while Shell fell 0.8%.

"The oil market is unlikely to return to the regime that prevailed before the conflict," Morgan Stanley said in a research note.

Morgan Stanley said the path to return to pre-conflict oil prices is "narrowing" as it raised its 2027 forecast Brent estimate to USD80 per barrel.

"Whatever path events take from here, the last four weeks have changed how investors must think about the Strait of Hormuz, effective spare capacity and the value of secure oil supply," the bank said.

"The market has now learned something that it cannot easily unlearn: the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz can in fact be cut off. Given the volume of oil involved, that amounts to a structural repricing of geopolitical risk in oil markets," it added.

The pound was quoted lower at USD1.3377 at the time of the London equities close on Wednesday, compared to USD1.3394 on Tuesday.

The euro stood at USD1.1572, lower against USD1.1591. Against the yen, the dollar was trading higher at JPY159.19 compared to JPY158.82.

The yield on the US 10-year Treasury narrowed to 4.32% from 4.38%. The yield on the US 30-year Treasury trimmed to 4.89% from 4.94% on Tuesday.

European Central Bank chief Christine Lagarde said the ECB has several options for dealing with the energy shock, vowing policymakers would not be "paralysed by hesitation".

At its most recent meeting last week, the ECB kept interest rates on hold, while warning of higher inflation and lower growth in the eurozone owing to the war.

On London's FTSE 100, Croda rose 3.3% as Morgan Stanley upgraded the speciality chemicals firm to 'overweight' from 'equal-weight'.

"Croda performs particularly well in an inflationary cycle," Morgan Stanley pointed out.

Endeavour Mining rose 4.5% as the gold price climbed. The yellow metal traded at USD4,554.59 per ounce on Wednesday, up against USD4,421.77 on Tuesday.

But Experian fell 1.4% after after US peer Fair Isaac Corp fell 6.4% in New York on Tuesday.

US Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte wrote on social media that "credit score pricing and credit bureau pricing must be more affordable". US Senator Josh Hawley said he is launching a probe into Fair Isaac. Experian currently charges for more detailed access to credit score information.

On the FTSE 250, RS Group fell 4.5% as it said full-year revenue is set to fall short of forecasts in "difficult" markets, although profit is likely to be marginally ahead of expectations.

Elsewhere, Asos stormed 13% higher as analysts said the online retailer was making progress as it maintained full-year guidance.

"We are constructive on Asos's ongoing recovery potential," said analysts at Berenberg, noting the sales trend is "heading in the right direction".

But Pinewood Technologies slid 7.7% as it delayed the roll-out of an artificial intelligence system to the second half of the year, and added that it will not meet its annual earnings target.

The biggest risers on the FTSE 100 were Endeavour Mining, up 184.00p at 4,312.00p, Pershing Square Holdings, up 158.00p at 4,128.00p, Anglo American, up 119.00p at 3,173.00p, GSK, up 77.00p at 2,054.00p and ICG, up 57.00p at 1,544.00p.

The biggest fallers on the FTSE 100 were Entain, down 10.60p at 573.80p, Relx, down 37.00p at 2,393.00p, Experian, down 36.00p at 2,530.00p, Bunzl, down 24.00p at 2,156.00p and Shell, down 27.00p at 3,433.00p.

Thursday's global economic calendar has consumer confidence reports in France and Germany, and US weekly initial jobless claims.

Thursday's UK corporate calendar has full-year results from clothing and homeware retailer Next.

By Jeremy Cutler, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2026 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

Canadian dollar hits two-month low as Middle East hopes weigh on oil
data.description:
TORONTO, March 25 (Reuters) - The Canadian dollar weakened to a two-month low against its U.S. counterpart on Wednesday as oil prices fell on hopes of a ceasefire in the Middle East conflict and investors weighed recent signs of domestic economic weakness.

The loonie was trading 0.3% lower at 1.3809 per U.S. dollar, or 72.42 U.S. cents, marking its weakest intraday level since January 22.

"The loonie's descent to a two-month low suggests that internal economic deceleration is now weighing more heavily on the currency than the support typically provided by favorable terms of trade in energy," said Kevin Ford, FX & macro strategist at Convera.

"Following a cautious tone from the Bank of Canada last week, investors have turned the focus back to a sluggish labor market and economic macro figures that have missed projections."

Canada's economy has been disrupted by hefty U.S. tariffs on critical sectors, such as autos, steel and aluminum. Exports were down 14.6% year-over-year in January and employment declined by 84,000 in February.

"Further pressure stems from geopolitical and trade-related uncertainties. While elevated energy prices are currently the primary pillar of support for the CAD, this makes the currency highly vulnerable to any de-escalation in Middle East tensions," Ford said.

Reports that the United States had sent Iran a 15-point proposal aimed at ending the war helped push U.S. crude oil futures 2.8% lower to $89.77 a barrel. Oil is one of Canada's major exports.

Last Wednesday, Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem said it was too early to assess the effect of the war as the central bank left its benchmark interest rate on hold at 2.25%.

Canadian bond yields moved lower across the curve. The 10-year was down 8.6 basis points at 3.483%, extending its pullback from Monday's near two-year high at 3.643%.

(Reporting by Fergal Smith; Editing by Chris Reese)

By Fergal Smith

BASF Raises Prices Sharply, Again, as Middle East War Drives Up Costs
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby


Chemicals giant BASF said it is hiking prices for more of its products in the face of an increase in costs due to the U.S. and Israel's war with Iran.

The German group said Wednesday that it is raising prices of commodity amines in Europe by up to 30%, with some products increasing by even more in price. Amines are used as solvents and catalysts in an array of industries, from pharmaceuticals to personal care and agrochemicals.

"These price increases are in response to the substantial increases in raw material prices, energy and logistic costs due to the military conflict in the Middle East," said BASF, one of the world's largest chemicals suppliers.

The company last week said it would raise prices by up to nearly a third, or in some cases more, for products in its home-care, industrial & institutional cleaning and industrial formulators segments in Europe. That followed a warning from Germany's chemicals association that the war in the Middle East had already begun to take its toll on the industry's supply chain, with an immediate spike in oil and gas prices spreading to other raw materials vital to chemical production.

The price-hikes will be implemented immediately, or as existing contracts allow, BASF said.


Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-25-26 1239ET

data.title: Visa Reports Joining Canton Network
Climate investors give BP until April 1 to include resolution or risk court action
data.description:
LONDON, March 25 (Reuters) - Climate activist shareholder group Follow This, together with a group of European investors with $1 trillion under management, said on Wednesday they have given BP until April 1 to include their resolution in the agenda for the oil major's shareholder meeting or they will take the firm to court.

* The group demanded BP disclose longer-term strategiesunder scenarios of declining oil and gas demand, but BP did notinclude the resolution in its notice of meeting. * Follow This said it has instructed law firm Mishcon deReya to send a formal letter to BP, giving the company one weekto include its resolution on the agenda at its annual generalmeeting. * In the letter, seen by Reuters, the law firm said: "Shouldthe board now still fail to do so, our client will be left withno option but to seek the assistance of the courts to order theboard to comply with its legal obligations." * BP's AGM is scheduled for April 23. * Follow This has filed a similar resolution for Shell's AGMscheduled for May 19. Shell has not published its agenda for themeeting yet. * Typically, it is much easier for shareholders in Europe toget resolutions on AGM agendas than in the U.S. * "This case is bigger than one resolution at one company,"said Follow This founder Mark van Baal. "Shareholder democracyin the UK is at stake. If BP can block a valid resolutionwithout explanation, any company can. We will not let thatstand." * A BP spokesperson said: "The board, having taken legaladvice, concluded that the proposal from Follow This was notvalid as it would be ineffective as a direction by shareholdersto the board were it to pass at the AGM. The board is clear thatall legally valid resolutions which are properly requisitionedwill be put to shareholders."

(Reporting by Shadia Nasralla. Editing by Mark Potter)

By Shadia Nasralla

data.description: On March 25, 2026, Colgate-Palmolive Company announced that it has received a shareholder proposal from National Legal and Policy Center, regarding a proposal on remove DEI from board candidate considerations. In addition, the Company recommended the shareholders to vote against the proposal at the annual meeting of shareholders scheduled to be held on May 8, 2026.

U.S. Unleashing 'Energy-Dominant' Strategy, Interior Secretary Says -- OPIS
data.description:
Interior Secretary Doug Burgum on Wednesday credited the Trump administration for progress in boosting Venezuela's oil production, improved relationships with allies and an "energy-dominant strategy."

In remarks at S&P Global's CERAWeek conference in Houston, Burgum said the "policy of energy transition was always false, because if you have a transition from reliable, secure and affordable, 24-hour dispatchable power to something that is intermittent, weather dependent and highly taxpayer subsidized it's not energy transition."

"It's energy subtraction at a time when we've got a demand signal for more power," he added.

Long permitting times have clogged the economy, but the secretary said the Trump administration has streamlined the permitting process, pointing to the completion of some environmental impact statements in as little as 24 days as two years under previous administrations. He said some environmental assessments have been finished in as few as 12 days.

"We're not saving the planet by killing U.S. industries and shoving them up around the world and creating highly insecure supply chains. We have to bring all of that home. Permitting is key to that."

Burgum said the U.S. should "unleash" its "extraordinary energy potential" to decrease dependence on global markets as well as to assist allies, highlighting South Korea and Japan's deep dependence on oil from the Persian Gulf .

"Our allies are completely vulnerable," Burgum said. "We have the ability to sell U.S. energy from Alaska. It'll keep the price down for the Europeans. It keeps the price down for Americans, but we can unleash all of that new supply to our allies in the Pacific and make it more secure."

He also said Venezuela, which had been a "hotbed of Iranian funded terror groups" is now a competitive and attractive investment opportunity.

Burgum said there are a number of companies with Venezuelan-American employees who know the region well and want to help develop the country's energy and mining sectors.

Now that the U.S. has removed former President Nicolas Maduro from power, current leader Delcy Rodríguez is receptive to cooperation from U.S. companies, he added.

Burgum said Delcy has asked U.S. companies interested in the county to "tell us what you want in the bill we're introducing on Saturday" and predicted Venezuela's oil output will rise by 50% from the start of 2026 in the near future.


This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.


Reporting by Bayan Raji, braji@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeffrey Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-25-26 1233ET
